title: Realistic Simulation of Quantum Repeater with Encoding and Classical Error Correction
authors: Sagar Patange, Caitao Zhan, Bikun Li, Joaquin Chung, Allen Zang, Liang Jiang, Rajkumar Kettimuthu
abstract: Quantum repeaters are essential for scalable long-distance quantum networking. As quantum information processing moves toward fault-tolerant and error-corrected operations, it becomes increasingly important to study quantum repeaters that also move beyond raw physical entanglement and towards logical entanglement. In this paper, we implement and simulate the quantum repeater with encoding and classical error correction (QRE-CEC) protocol in SeQUeNCe, a discrete-event simulator of quantum networks. The protocol distributes logical Bell pairs, performs encoded entanglement swapping, and uses classical error correction for the decoding of entanglement swapping measurement outcomes to determine Pauli-frame corrections. For this study, we extend SeQUeNCe with a stabilizer-based backend, add support for CSS code-based encoded operations, and integrate gate, measurement, idle decoherence, and state-initialization noise models. Our simulation results show that QRE-CEC suppresses all modeled errors to the second order. Also, QRE-CEC can distribute logical Bell pairs with 0.91 fidelity over a distance of 2000 km under the parameter regimes we study. Beyond protocol-level performance evaluation, our implementation exposes practical simulator and control-plane challenges that are typically abstracted away in theoretical studies.
summary: This paper presents a realistic simulation of a quantum repeater protocol that combines quantum encoding (Steane code) with classical error correction. The study demonstrates that this approach can suppress errors to the second order, enabling high-fidelity entanglement distribution over continental distances of 2000 km.
main_problem: Evaluating the practical performance of the Quantum Repeater with Encoding and Classical Error Correction (QRE-CEC) protocol under realistic, system-level conditions, specifically bridging the gap between theoretical protocols and realistic implementation.
main_result: The QRE-CEC protocol suppresses modeled errors to the second order and can maintain a logical Bell pair fidelity of 0.91 over a distance of 2000 km, significantly outperforming protocols without classical error correction.
method: The authors extended the SeQUeNCe discrete-event simulator with a stabilizer-based backend using the Stim toolkit to efficiently simulate CSS code-based operations and noise models.
model_or_system: A linear network of second-generation quantum repeaters using the Steane [7, 1, 3] code, featuring communication, data, and ancilla qubits, with noise models including depolarizing gates, photon loss, and idling decoherence.
key_observables: End-to-end logical Bell-pair fidelity, total protocol latency, and logical error rate suppression.
important_parameters: Inter-node distance (up to 2000 km), number of links (up to 100), gate fidelities, coherence times (T1, T2), and fiber attenuation.
main_assumptions: The study assumes a specific 2G-type repeater architecture using the Steane code and focuses on the ability to derive idling decoherence from T1 and T2 parameters.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the internal repeater structure; Figure 2 details the five phases of the QRE-CEC protocol; Figure 3 shows the Steane encoder circuit; Figure 4 details the gate teleportation circuit; Figure 5 illustrates the SeQUeNCe simulator extensions; Figure 6 shows error suppression scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QRE-CEC protocol, describes the implementation of the extended SeQUeNCe simulator, details the physical noise models and hardware parameters, presents simulation results regarding fidelity and latency across various network scales, and discusses the trade-offs between circuit depth and error suppression.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it addresses the practical challenges of maintaining entanglement in the presence of realistic decoherence and hardware noise in large-scale quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. A quantum repeater with encoding has three types of qubits: communication qubits (gray), data qubits (blue), and ancilla qubits (red).

Fig. 2. The QRE-CEC protocol. (a) Phases one through three: heralded entanglement generation, fault-tolerant logical state preparation, and logical teleported CNOT, which together produce encoded Bell pairs between neigh- boring nodes. (b) All five phases of QRE-CEC, adding encoded swapping with classical error correction and Pauli-frame correction to generate end-to- end logical Bell pairs.
Fig. 5. Illustration of new modules introduced to SeQUeNCe to fully support the implementation of a quantum repeater with encoding and error correction.

Fig. 6. Logical Bell-pair fidelity under sweeps of six physical error parameters for linear topologies with 1, 2, and 5 links with 20 km link distance. In each sweep, all other noise sources are set to zero. Subfigures (a)–(f) show the logical Bell-pair fidelity as a function of two-qubit gate fidelity, idling decoherence time T2, physical Bell-pair fidelity, one-qubit gate fidelity, measurement fidelity, and initialization fidelity, respectively. In (a)–(f), the y = x line marks the threshold: points above it mean the end-to-end logical fidelity exceeds the physical fidelity parameter being swept. Subfigures (g)–(l) show the corresponding logical error rate versus the associated physical...

Fig. 7. End-to-End Logical Bell-pair Fidelity versus the hardware sweep parameter z for 1-, 2-, and 5-link linear repeater chains with fixed 20 km inter- node spacing. Higher z corresponds to uniformly better hardware performance across all swept parameters. The right panel zooms into the near-perfect- fidelity regime.
